coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots from triflic acid treated palm shell waste and their application in nitrophenol sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Soni, Harnish
Pamidimukkala, Padmaja Sudhakar
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report N and S co-doped Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) prepared using palm shell powder (PSP) and triflic acid. The CQDs possessed a graphitic-like structure with narrow size distribution and exhibited a bright green fluorescence. The fluorescent property of CQDs was found to be independent of the excitation wavelength and was used as fluorescence probe for nitrophenols (NPs) with good detection limits (0.079 μM, 0.165 μM, 0.082 μM for NP, DNP and TNP respectively) and linearity in the range 0.2–0.40 μM, 0.5–85 μM, 0.20–40 μM for NP, DNP and TNP respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Palm shell waste
Fluorescence
Nitrophenolsls

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to Head, Department of Chemistry for providing necessary facilities to carry out this work. The author Harnish Soni is thankful to University Grants commission , Delhi, India, for UGC-BSR fellowship as financial assistance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CQDs (b) FTIR spectrum of CQDs (c) Raman spectrum of CQDs (d) XPS survey spectra of CQDs; Inset of (a) Shows expanded view of CQD image.
(a) Absorption spectra of CQDs (b) Emission spectra of CQDs on excitation at different wavelengths (280–430 nm).

Fluorescence of CQDs in the presence of (a) 4-NP (b) DNP (C) TNP (measured at 410 nm excitation and 310 nm emission wavelength) (d) Quenching of CQDs fluorescence in presence of different analytes under study (2 ml CQDs+ 497.5 μM analyte).
Stern-Volmer Constants and Coefficients of Determination.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots from triflic acid treated palm shell waste and their application in nitrophenol sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, The Maharaja Sayajirao University of Baroda, Vadodara, 390 002, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Maharaja Sayajirao University of Baroda

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report N and S co-doped Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) prepared using palm shell powder (PSP) and triflic acid. The CQDs possessed a graphitic-like structure with narrow size distribution and exhibited a bright green fluorescence. The fluorescent property of CQDs was found to be independent of the excitation wavelength and was used as fluorescence probe for nitrophenols (NPs) with good detection limits (0.079 μM, 0.165 μM, 0.082 μM for NP, DNP and TNP respectively) and linearity in the range 0.2–0.40 μM, 0.5–85 μM, 0.20–40 μM for NP, DNP and TNP respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrophenols are widely used in synthesis of various products including dyes, pesticides and pharmaceuticals [1]. Nitrophenols (NPs) are categorized by U.S. Environmental Protection Agency as hazardous environmental contaminants thus requiring rapid and sensitive methods for their detection [2].
Carbon dots (CDs) are newly emerging carbon nanomaterials, that have applications in various fields, including bio-imaging, sensing, photovoltaic devices and catalysis [3].

Carbon dots (CDs) mainly include carbon nano particles of size less than 10 nm, also called carbon quantum dots, CQDs [4]. They exhibit unique optical properties such as fluorescence, chemiluminescence and electrochemiluminescence due to their quantum confinement and edge effects. Furthermore, they present many advantages including low toxicity, low cost, robust chemical and optical inertness over semiconductor based quantum dots [5]. Till date, many simple and efficient methods have been proposed to synthesize different types of CDs. The methods can be generally classified as “bottom-up” and “top-down”. The “bottom-up” strategies involve carbonizing some precursors through hydrothermal [6],thermal [7], microwave [8] techniques, acid oxidation [9], electrochemical or sonication treatments [10]. Usually, these “bottom-up” strategies provide advantages such as precise control over the morphology and the size distribution of the product [11] or convenience for surface-passivation or heteroatom doping to prepare high yield luminescent materials [6].

“Top-down” techniques for CQDs involve cutting some big-size carbon sources such as graphite [12], activated carbon [13,14], graphene or graphene oxide [15] carbon nanotubes [16], carbon fibres [17] and carbon black [18].

However, these “bottom-up’’ methods are time consuming and include the need to optimize the hydrothermal treatment, temperature control and precursor selection [19]. However, most reported precursors used in both “bottom-up” and “top-down” methods are either too expensive or difficult to obtain and are not suitable for mass production.

Recently the preparation of CQDs using nontoxic solvents and economical green materials has gained prominence. The synthetic strategies adapted include sugarcane stalk [20], Carica papaya juice [21], Neem gum [22], Mannose and ammonium citrate [23], Trapabispinosa peel [24], Coffee grounds [25], Apple juice [26], Glucose [27], Anthracite [28], Orange juice and ethylenediamine [29], Chicken egg [30], Gelatin [31], Chitosan [32], Diesel engine soot [33], Glucosamine [34], Ascorbic acid [35], Wheat straw [36], Banana juice [37], Saccharum officinarum juice [38], Soya-nuggets [39], Cucumber/Pineapple waste [40], Jinhua bergamot [41], Candle soot [42] and sugarcane bagasse pulp carbon [43].
To the best of our knowledge, there are very few reports related to CQDs as sensors for determination of nitrophenols [44].

Triflic acid (Trifluoromethanesulfonic acid, TFA) is a strong non-oxidizing bronsted superacid, extensively utilized in acid catalyzed synthetic transformations [45].Our objective was to synthesize CQDs by treating palm shell powder(PSP) with triflic acid and investigate their ability to be used as sensors for the detection of nitrophenols.

Dried PSP was treated with TFA (Sp. gr. 1.7) in the ratio 1:1.5 of PSP to TFA and digested for 10 h in an oven at 150 °C. The resultant char obtained was washed with water followed by 2% solution of NaHCO3 until effervescences ceased and was further left to soak in a 2% solution of NaHCO3 overnight. The TFA activated palm shell char was then separated, washed with water until free of bicarbonate and dried at 105 °C (TFAC).

About 0.5 g of TFAC was ultrasonicated in 50 mL conductivity water for 30 min and filtered through whatmann filter paper 42. The filtrate was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove heavier particles. The obtained light brown color solution was purified using dialysis membrane (6000–8000 Da) to obtain CQDs which exhibited a bright green colour fluorescence (Fig. 2a) under ultraviolet irradiation. This is the first ever attempt to use TFA for the preparation of CQDs.

The CQDs were characterized by FTIR (Perkin-Elmer RX1 model), TEM (Tecnai-12, FEI-Netherlands), Raman (B&W TEK), XPS (EA 125D), UV (JASCO, V-630 model) and fluorescence (JASCO, FP-6300) spectroscopic techniques.

The potential of prepared CQDs as sensors for NPs, phenol, MnCP and 4-CP was evaluated. The study was performed by measuring fluorescence intensity of CQDs solution in presence of nitrophenol. In a typical experiment, 2 mL of 10% CQD solution was take in a quartz cuvette and initial fluorescence intensity was measured. Further each analyte under study of desired concentration was added to the cuvette containing 2 mL of 10% CQD solution and fluorescence intensity was again measured.

TEM images reveal the formation of well dispersed CQDs with size in the range 4–10 nm (Fig. 1a ). The sharp band at ∼3350 cm−1 observed in the FT-IR spectra of CQDs (Fig. 1b) corresponded to the stretching vibrations of OH groups, whereas the peak centred at ∼2900 cm−1 is typically associated with CH stretching vibrations and the peak at ∼ 3200 cm−1 may be attributed to NH stretching vibration. The CQDs also exhibited bands at ∼3050 cm−1 due to stretching vibration of C–H in aromatic rings and at ∼1550 cm−1 due to skeletal vibrations of aromatic rings. The broad peak centred at ∼1600 cm−1 may be attributed to CC / CO symmetric stretching vibrations while the bands observed at ∼1400 cm−1 and 1063 cm−1 may be attributed to CO and CO/CF stretching vibrations respectively [46]. Further the band at ∼1500 cm−1 can be attributed to CN stretching vibrations [47]. The stretching vibrations of thiophene ring can be seen in the range 800–840 cm−1.
The Raman spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 1c) exhibited a major G-band (sp2 carbon) at ∼1633 cm−1. The G signal was broad suggesting different populations of CC bonds with varying levels of strain [48].

The shift of the G band by about 33 cm−1 might be attributed to the small sizes of the basal graphene layers in the grains of the CQDs. The presence of a very weak D band at 1326.17 cm−1 suggests highly ordered graphene like structure with hexagonal aromatic rings [49]. The Id/Ig ratio was found to be 0.2519 which also supported graphene like structure of the CQDs. The peaks at 432 and 500 cm−1 are attributed to bending modes of sp and sp3 bonded carbon respectively [50].

Fig. 1d depicts the XPS survey spectrum of CQDs. The atomic percent was measured to be 47%, 45%, 1%, 7% and 6% for C, O, N, F and S atoms respectively (Fig. S1). The C1s XPS spectrum can be fitted into four Gaussian peaks at 284.65, 286.01, 288.35 and 291.6 eV, which are attributed to CC/CS, COC/ CN, OCO and CF bonds respectively [51,52]. The N1 s XPS signal at 400.0 eV may be attributed to pyrrolic N [53]. The O 1 s spectrum has two peaks centered at 531.54 eV and 535.65 eV which could be attributed to CO and CO respectively. The sulfur was observed to be thiophenic with the signal at 163.9 ev [53] and the F1 s peak was observed at 687.85 eV. Detailed attribution of the binding energies is given in Table S1.
The CQDs (Fig. 2a) exhibited broad absorption in the UV and visible region with a shoulder at 278 nm attributed to π-π* electron transitions.

The CQDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence (Fig. 2b), [54]. The emission spectra maximum was observed to shift from 320 nm to 565 nm as the excitation wavelength was varied from 280 nm to 430 nm. The maximum emission intensity was observed at ∼410 nm (bright green emission) when excited at 310 nm; exhibiting a stoke shift of 100 nm [55]. The fluorescence intensity increased with pH increment up to pH 5, was constant in the pH range 6–8 and decreased slightly with further increment in pH till pH 11. This change in fluorescent intensity may be attributed to protonation/ deprotonation and charge transfer involved with different type of functional groups present on CQDs (Fig. S2). The CQDs have different kinds of functional groups like CH, CO, COC, COH, CSC as well as CNH2, on the surface which could form a series of energy levels in surface states. These energy levels might result in formation of emissive traps [56].

The potential of CQDs to sense 4-NP (4-nitrophenol), DNP (2,4-dinitrophenol), TNP (2,4,6-trinitrophenol), 4-CP (4-chlorophenol), BPA (Bisphenol-A) and MnCP (Monocrotophos) was further investigated. It can be seen from (Fig. 3 a–c) that the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs was gradually quenched upon the addition of NP, DNP and TNP with maximum quenching occurring at concentrations of 0.75 mM, 0.99 mM and 0.69 mM respectively. It was observed that Nitrophenols were better detected as compared to other analytes under study (Fig. 3d).

The selective fluorescent quenching can probably be attributed to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the excited electrons of CQDs (energy donor) and electron deficient nitrophenols (energy acceptor) [57–59]. Nitrophenols have a wide absorption peak between 300–450 nm while the CQDs exhibited maximum emission at 410 nm.

The performance of the CQDs were analysed using Stern-Volmer equation [60] and the parameters are listed in Table S1. A linear plot of F0/F vs [Q] ((where F0 is fluorescence intensity in the absence of the quencher, F the fluorescence intensity in the presence of quencher, and Q the concentration of quencher) (Fig. 3(a–c)) was obtained.

The KSV (Stern-Volmer quenching constant) values obtained from the slope indicated the order of sensitivity of the CQDs for the analytes under study (Table 1 ). The larger the KSV value, the more sensitive the CQDs are to that particular analyte. Further, from the plots the Limits of detection (LOD) and Limits of Quantitation (LOQ) were calculated for each analyte under study. The linear range of the CQD sensor for nitrophenols was observed to be 0.2–0.40 μM, 0.5–85 μM and 0.20–40 μM for NP, DNP and TNP respectively while LOD was calculated as 0.079 μM, 0.165 μM and 0.082 μM for NP, DNP and TNP respectively. The preformance characteristics of the prepared CQDS were comparable with literature reported nano carbon based sensors for nitrophenols (Table S2).

Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CQDs were successfully prepared using palm shell powder and triflic acid. The as-prepared CQDs exhibited good dispersibility, strong photoluminescence and a rapid, sensitive and selective response towards nitrophenols over other organic pollutants like phenol, p-Chlorophenol and monocrotophos. The linear range of the CQD sensor foe nireophenols was 0.2–0.40 μM, 0.5–85 μM, 0.20–40 μM for NP, DNP and TNP respectively with LOD of 0.079 μM, 0.165 μM, 0.082 μM for NP, DNP and TNP respectively.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to Head, Department of Chemistry for providing necessary facilities to carry out this work. The author Harnish Soni is thankful to University Grants commission, Delhi, India, for UGC-BSR fellowship as financial assistance

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.materresbull.2018.08.033.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix C
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
